schools.summary: Victorinus: the first seal being opened, he saw a white horse and a crowned horseman having a bow; the Lamb who alone could open the seals now begins to unfold the sealed book; John seeing the Lamb open one of the seals, and hearing, as the noise of thunder, one of the four living creatures say 'Come and see' — the Lamb beginning to unloose the sealed book of God's purposes (John, having seen the Lamb take the book, now beholding him 'open one of the seals,' the Lamb alone worthy and able to unfold the hidden counsels of God concerning the church and the world, and at the opening 'one of the four beasts,' living creatures, crying 'as the noise of thunder, Come and see,' a loud, commanding summons to behold the vision that follows; the solemn beginning of the unsealing, that the future is opened only by the Lamb, and the heavenly creatures call the seer to attend to each great scene as it is disclosed).

themes: and I saw when the Lamb opened one of the seals
one of the four beasts saying, Come and see
the Lamb alone unfolding God's hidden counsels
the creatures' thunderous summons to behold

schools.summary: Victorinus: the white horse and crowned horseman with a bow signify the word of God, going forth in the days of the apostles to conquer, the Holy Spirit carried by the preaching to the nations; Gill: the white horse represents the ministration of the gospel in the apostles' times (which were just finishing, John being the last apostle to see this vision), the horse being swift, majestic, warlike, and fearless of opposition, white denoting the purity of the gospel and the victory it gained; John seeing a white horse, its rider with a bow and a crown, going forth conquering and to conquer — the triumphant going-forth of the conquering rider (John seeing the first seal disclose a vision of conquest: 'a white horse,' white the colour of purity and victory, 'and he that sat on him had a bow; and a crown was given unto him: and he went forth conquering, and to conquer'; the interpretive variety the commentators allow, some, with Victorinus and Gill, taking the rider to be Christ in the victorious going-forth of his gospel in the apostolic age, conquering hearts by the word, others reading the rider as Christ himself or his cause triumphing through the ages, all agreeing the scene is one of victory, a crowned conqueror riding forth in unstoppable triumph).

debates.question: Who is the rider on the white horse of the first seal?

sides.position: Christ in the victorious going-forth of his gospel in the apostolic age, conquering hearts by the word
Christ himself or his cause triumphing through the ages — a scene of victory however applied

themes: and I saw, and behold a white horse
he that sat on him had a bow; and a crown was given unto him
he went forth conquering, and to conquer
the gospel's victorious going-forth

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the next three seals give us a sad prospect of great and desolating judgments with which God punishes those who either refuse or abuse the everlasting gospel; Gill notes 'when he had opened the second seal,' that is the Lamb, as before; John hearing the second living creature say 'Come and see' as the Lamb opened the second seal — the second creature summoning the seer to the next vision of judgment (John, as the Lamb 'had opened the second seal,' hearing 'the second beast say, Come and see,' a fresh summons from a different one of the four living creatures to behold the next scene; the orderly unfolding, that each seal is opened by the Lamb in turn and at each a heavenly creature calls the seer to attend, and Matthew Henry noting the turn that now begins, that after the bright vision of the conquering gospel the next three seals open a darker prospect of judgments upon those who reject or abuse the gospel, war, famine, and death, so the second creature's summons introduces the first of these woes).

themes: and when he had opened the second seal
I heard the second beast say, Come and see
the second creature summoning to the next vision
a darker prospect of judgment beginning

schools.summary: Gill: the red horse may be an emblem either of the suffering state of the church (answerable to the Smyrnaean church, as the purity and power of the gospel in the former seal answers to the Ephesine), or else of the contentions, wars, and slaughters that arose; its rider was given power to take peace from the earth; John seeing a red horse whose rider was given power to take peace from the earth and make men kill one another, with a great sword — the red horse bringing war, bloodshed, and the loss of peace (the second seal disclosing a fearful scene of strife: 'another horse that was red,' red the colour of blood and slaughter, 'and power was given to him that sat thereon to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one another: and there was given unto him a great sword'; the meaning the commentators draw, that the going-out of this red horse signifies the coming of war, bloodshed, and mutual slaughter, peace being taken from the earth so men turn their swords against one another, whether read of the persecutions of the church or the bloody convulsions of the empire, the scene being one of violence and the great sword of carnage).

themes: and there went out another horse that was red
power was given to him... to take peace from the earth
that they should kill one another: and there was given unto him a great sword
the red horse of war and bloodshed

schools.summary: Barnes: the third living creature takes a part in calling attention to the new vision; the black horse and the balances signify scarcity, the careful weighing of food in time of famine, when bread is dealt out by weight; Gill notes the opening of the third seal of the sealed book; John seeing, at the third seal, a black horse whose rider held a pair of balances — the black horse and balances betokening scarcity and famine (the third seal opening a vision of want: at the summons of 'the third beast,' John beholding 'a black horse,' black the colour of mourning and distress, 'and he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand'; the symbol, that the 'balances,' scales, in the rider's hand betoken scarcity and dearth, for in time of famine food is doled out by careful weight, measured and rationed, the black colour fitting the gloom and grief of want, so the third seal, following war, brings the famine that war breeds, food weighed out in scant measure).

themes: and when he had opened the third seal... lo a black horse
he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand
the black horse and balances betokening scarcity
famine, food weighed out in scant measure

schools.summary: Gill: the voice in the midst of the four beasts is rather that of Christ, who was in the midst of them (not the voice of Agabus foretelling a famine to Paul); the words declare the dearth and scarcity of provisions, a measure of wheat (a day's allowance) for a penny (a day's wages); John hearing a voice declare a measure of wheat for a penny and three of barley for a penny, yet bidding hurt not the oil and the wine — the high famine-prices of grain, with a measure of mercy in the sparing of oil and wine (the vision of the third seal completed by a voice, which Gill takes to be Christ's, 'in the midst of the four beasts,' proclaiming the famine-prices: 'a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny,' a day's wage spent for a single day's bread, the very mark of scarcity and dearth, yet with a limit set: 'see thou hurt not the oil and the wine'; the touch of mercy in the judgment, that though grain is scarce and dear, the 'oil and the wine' are spared, so the famine, severe as it is, is not utter and total destruction, God's judgments even in the dearth being tempered and bounded).

themes: a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny
see thou hurt not the oil and the wine
the famine-prices declared, a day's wage for a day's bread
the judgment tempered, the oil and wine spared

schools.summary: Barnes: as in the previous cases, the fourth living creature, the flying eagle, calls attention to the new vision now to be disclosed; Gill notes 'when he had opened the fourth seal,' that is when the Lamb had opened it; John hearing the voice of the fourth living creature say 'Come and see' as the Lamb opened the fourth seal — the fourth creature summoning the seer to the next scene of judgment (John, at the opening of 'the fourth seal,' hearing 'the voice of the fourth beast,' the fourth of the living creatures, the flying eagle, 'say, Come and see,' the same orderly summons as before, now from the last of the four creatures; the continuing pattern, that each of the four living creatures in turn has called the seer to behold a seal opened, and this fourth and final summons introduces the most terrible of the four horsemen, the pale horse of death that now follows, so the regular solemn unfolding goes on, the fourth creature opening the way to the climax of the four riders).

themes: and when he had opened the fourth seal
I heard the voice of the fourth beast say, Come and see
the fourth creature summoning to the next scene
introducing the pale horse of death

schools.summary: Gill: the pale horse may be an emblem either of the state of the church (pale, not with persecution, for through that it was red, but with the hypocrisy and superstition of many of its members who were paving the way for the man of sin), or of a time of great mortality; its rider's name was Death, and Hell (the grave or hades) followed with him; John seeing a pale horse, its rider named Death with Hell following, given power over a fourth of the earth to kill by sword, hunger, death, and beasts — the pale horse of death gathering the previous plagues into one fearful judgment (the fourth seal disclosing the most dreadful vision: 'a pale horse,' pale the colour of a corpse, of sickness and death, 'and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him,' Death the rider, the grave or hades following to swallow the slain, 'and power was given unto them over the fourth part of the earth, to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts of the earth'; this rider gathering up all the previous plagues, sword the second seal, hunger the third, pestilence 'death,' and wild beasts, into one comprehensive judgment sweeping away a 'fourth part of the earth,' yet even here the destruction limited to a fourth, the judgment bounded by God's hand).

themes: and behold a pale horse: and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him
power was given unto them over the fourth part of the earth
to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts
the pale horse gathering all the plagues, yet bounded

schools.summary: Gill: this was a very dark time both with respect to the church of God and the ministry of the word; here is no beast speaking nor horse and rider presented, but the souls of the martyrs under the altar, slain for the word of God and the testimony they held; John seeing, at the fifth seal, under the altar the souls of those slain for the word of God and their testimony — the martyrs' souls beneath the altar, conscious and alive after death (the fifth seal, unlike the first four, showing no horse and rider but a scene in heaven: 'I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God, and for the testimony which they held'; two things, first the place and state of the martyrs, their 'souls' under the altar, a place of honour and safety near God, the altar speaking of their sacrifice, seen as living conscious souls after death, proving the soul does not perish or sleep with the body, and second the cause of their death, they were 'slain for the word of God, and for the testimony which they held,' martyred not for crime but for the faith they confessed, so the fifth seal honours the holy martyrs whose souls live with God).

debates.question: What does the fifth seal teach about the state of the soul after death?

sides.position: The martyrs are seen as living, conscious souls under the altar — proving the soul does not perish or sleep with the body but lives with God after death

themes: and when he had opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain
for the word of God
and for the testimony which they held
the martyrs' souls living, conscious, near God

schools.summary: Gill: they cried with great ardour and fervency, being very pressing and importunate, which shows that they were awake and not asleep, and that the soul does not sleep with the body in the grave or remain after death in a state of insensibility and inactivity; John hearing the souls cry with a loud voice, 'How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood?' — the martyrs' importunate cry for God's righteous judgment on their persecutors (the souls under the altar heard to cry 'with a loud voice,' earnest and importunate, proof again that they live and are conscious, 'How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?'; the spirit of the cry, that it is no private vindictiveness but an appeal to God as 'holy and true,' whose holiness must punish sin and whose truth must keep his word, for the righteous avenging of the innocent blood shed by their persecutors, the 'how long' voicing the longing of the suffering church for the day of God's vindication, when he will judge the world and avenge his saints).

themes: and they cried with a loud voice, How long, O Lord, holy and true
dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?
the martyrs' cry for righteous judgment
the longing of the suffering church for God's vindication

schools.summary: Gill: white robes were given to every one of them (some copies read 'a white robe') as a token of their innocence, that they did not suffer or were not slain for any capital crime or immorality they were guilty of, but were righteous in God's sight; and a token of their present blessedness and victory; John seeing white robes given to each martyr, and they were bidden rest yet a little season until the full number of their fellow-martyrs should be completed — the answer to the martyrs' cry, robes of honour now, vindication when the number is full (John recording God's gracious answer to the martyrs' 'how long': first, 'white robes were given unto every one of them,' a token of their innocence, acceptance, and present blessedness, the white speaking of their purity and victory, and second, a word of patience, 'it was said unto them, that they should rest yet for a little season, until their fellowservants also and their brethren, that should be killed as they were, should be fulfilled'; the gracious delay, that the full avenging is not yet but only 'a little season,' for God has a fixed number of martyrs still to be slain before the day of his vindication, so the martyrs are comforted with present honour and rest and assured the day of reckoning will surely come when their number is complete).

themes: and white robes were given unto every one of them
they should rest yet for a little season
until their fellowservants... should be fulfilled
robes of honour now, vindication when the number is full

schools.summary: Gill: interpreters much differ about this seal; some refer it to the destruction of Jerusalem (because of the like signs mentioned by Christ), but it is more largely and probably applied to the great shaking and overthrow of the heathen Roman empire, beginning under Constantine; John seeing, at the sixth seal, a great earthquake, the sun blackened, and the moon turned to blood — the convulsion of the heavens and earth at the sixth seal, a vision of overturning judgment (the sixth seal opening a scene of cosmic upheaval: 'a great earthquake,' a violent shaking, the very frame of things convulsed, 'and the sun became black as sackcloth of hair, and the moon became as blood'; the interpretive variety the commentators set out, some referring this convulsion to the destruction of Jerusalem, others to the day of judgment, but many, Gill and Barnes, to the great overthrow of the pagan Roman empire, the 'earthquake' and the darkening of the great lights of heaven figuring the fall of rulers and the collapse of the old order, whichever the application, the imagery being of God's awful judgment shaking the established powers to their foundations).

debates.question: What does the sixth seal's cosmic upheaval represent?

sides.position: The overthrow of the pagan Roman empire (under Constantine) — the earthquake and darkened lights figuring the fall of rulers and the old order

themes: and I beheld when he had opened the sixth seal... a great earthquake
the sun became black as sackcloth of hair, and the moon became as blood
the convulsion of heaven and earth at the sixth seal
God's judgment shaking the powers to their foundations

schools.summary: Gill: in the historicist application, all the other inferior deities lost their esteem, worship, and honour, for the idol temples being now opened the idols and statues were exposed to the common people and found to be stuffed with hay and straw; the stars represent those cast down from their dignity, princes and dignitaries; John seeing the stars of heaven fall to earth like untimely figs shaken from a tree by a mighty wind — the falling stars figuring the casting down of the high and exalted (the sixth seal's upheaval continuing: 'the stars of heaven fell unto the earth, even as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs, when she is shaken of a mighty wind'; the vivid figure, that as a fig tree violently shaken by a great wind drops its unripe figs in a shower to the ground, so the 'stars of heaven' fall, in the prophetic language 'stars' standing for the high, the rulers, princes, and dignitaries, their falling figuring the casting down from their place of all that was exalted and bright in the old order, the great ones brought low in the day of God's judgment, so nothing high stands when God shakes the heavens).

themes: and the stars of heaven fell unto the earth
as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs, when she is shaken of a mighty wind
the falling stars figuring the high cast down
nothing high standing when God shakes the heavens

schools.summary: Gill: the manner of writing formerly was on rolls of parchment, hence the word 'volume' is used for a book, which when rolled up the writing within could not be seen; in like manner (in the historicist reading) the Roman empire's heaven departed and was rolled up; John seeing the heaven depart as a scroll rolled together, and every mountain and island moved out of its place — the total upheaval, the very heavens removed and the firmest things displaced (the sixth seal reaching its height: 'the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together,' the visible heaven rolled up and removed as a parchment scroll is rolled shut and put away, 'and every mountain and island were moved out of their places'; the completeness of the overturning, that not only are sun, moon, and stars darkened and fallen but the very 'heaven' is rolled away, and 'every mountain and island,' the most fixed, stable, immovable things, is shaken 'out of their places,' in the figure the whole established order from the highest to the most settled utterly removed at God's judgment, so the imagery declares that nothing, however fixed, can stand before the day of his wrath).

themes: and the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together
every mountain and island were moved out of their places
the total upheaval, the firmest things displaced
nothing standing before the day of God's wrath

schools.summary: Barnes: the design of this and the following verses is to depict the universal consternation, the dismay that would seize upon all classes of men; Gill: the kings of the earth are the Roman emperors and other principal magistrates, governors of provinces and cities; John seeing the kings, great men, rich, captains, mighty, and every bondman and free man hide in the dens and rocks of the mountains — the universal terror in which men of every rank seek to hide from God's wrath (the sixth seal's judgment falling on all sorts of men, and John naming them in a sweeping list from the highest to the lowest: 'the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief captains, and the mighty men, and every bondman, and every free man'; the universality and the terror, that no rank or class is exempt, neither king nor slave, and all alike, struck with dread, 'hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains,' seeking any shelter from the coming wrath, so the day of God's judgment levels all distinctions, and the mighty and the lowly are filled with one common terror, fleeing to hide).

themes: and the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief captains
every bondman, and every free man
hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains
the day of judgment levelling all distinctions in terror

schools.summary: Gill: they chose death rather than life, calling on the mountains and rocks to fall on them, so great was their terror and despair (the historian noting Diocletian, invited by Constantine to a feast, excusing himself and, receiving threatening letters, choosing death); John hearing them cry to the mountains and rocks, 'Fall on us, and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb' — the despair that prefers death to facing the wrath of God and the Lamb (the terror reaching such a height that men cry to the very mountains and rocks to crush them, 'Fall on us, and hide us'; the depth of their despair, that they would rather be buried alive under falling rocks than face 'him that sitteth on the throne,' God the Judge, and 'the wrath of the Lamb'; the striking phrase 'the wrath of the Lamb,' that the very One who is meek and gentle, the Lamb slain, is now the object of terror, for his wrath, when at last it comes, is the more dreadful for the patience and gentleness so long despised, so the wicked, who would not bow to the Lamb's grace, now flee in horror from the Lamb's wrath).

themes: and said to the mountains and rocks, Fall on us, and hide us
from the face of him that sitteth on the throne
and from the wrath of the Lamb
the meek Lamb's wrath dreadful for grace despised

schools.summary: Gill: the great day of his wrath is come, both of him that sits upon the throne and of the Lamb, against the heathen gods and heathen persecutors, in answer to the cry of the martyrs (the Vulgate and Syriac reading 'of their wrath'); John recording the cry, 'For the great day of his wrath is come; and who shall be able to stand?' — the terrible day of wrath arrived, before which none can stand (the despairing flight of the wicked closed with the reason and the unanswerable question: 'for the great day of his wrath is come,' the long-delayed day of God's and the Lamb's righteous anger having at last arrived in answer to the martyrs' 'how long,' 'and who shall be able to stand?'; the force of the question, that it is a cry of utter hopelessness, owning that none can endure or survive that day, none can stand before the wrath of the almighty God and the Lamb, the very persecutors who once seemed all-powerful now confessing they cannot stand, so the sixth seal ends with the overthrow of the proud and the demonstration that no power can resist the day of God's wrath, a solemn answer to the martyrs' prayer and a warning to all).

themes: for the great day of his wrath is come
and who shall be able to stand?
the great day of wrath arrived
none able to endure the wrath of God and the Lamb
